question.text: A friend tells Mr. Joshi: "This fund gave 50% return last year, so it will give 50% this year too." What is wrong with this logic?

answer.text: Past performance does not guarantee future results — markets can change
Nothing is wrong — past returns always repeat
The return should have been higher than 50%
Only funds with 100% return are reliable

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Past performance does not guarantee future results. Market conditions, economic factors, interest rates, and many other variables change over time. A fund that performed well last year may or may not repeat that performance.
**The Trap:** Assuming past returns will repeat is one of the most common and dangerous investor mistakes. It leads to unrealistic expectations and poor investment decisions.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Also tested in Series V-A. The "past performance" disclaimer is one of the most important concepts in mutual fund investing.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When investors chase last year's top performers, gently remind them of this principle. Help them focus on consistent long-term performance rather than one-year wonders.
